coredata.dc:title: Chiral helical supramolecular hydrogels with adjustable pitch and diameter towards high-performance chiroptical detecting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Li
Su, Nan
Huang, Juexin
Dou, Xiaoqiu
Zhao, Changli
Feng, Chuanliang
coredata.dc:description: Helical architectures with adjustable helical parameters and chiroptical functions are expected to play a fundamental role in biomedicine and nanoscience fields but is still in its early stage of study. Herein, chiral phenylalanine-derived hydrogelators (DG1 and LG1) can self-assemble into left-handed or right-handed helical nanofibrous hydrogels. Achiral carbon dots with different charges are co-assembled with DG1 or LG1 through electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds, helical supramolecular hydrogels with controllable helical pitch and diameter are readily obtained. Typically, the co-assembled hydrogels show both circular dichroism (CD) and circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) signals in the absorption regions. The nanofibers with the largest helical pitch and smallest diameter show the highest luminescence dissymmetry g-factors (g lum) up to -3.80 × 10−3, which are able to efficiently differentiate between left and right circularly polarized light. This work not only paves a universal method to obtain chiral helical nanostructures with adjustable pitch and diameter, but also shines light on these advanced helical nanostructures for potential applications in chiroptical detectors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Self-assembly
Chiral supramolecular hydrogels
Adjustable pitch and diameter
Circularly polarized luminescence
Chiroptical detecting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Controllable pitch and diameter of helical nanofibers in supramolecular hydrogels
Chiroptical activity of helical supramolecular hydrogels
The driving forces for the construction of chiral helical supramolecular hydrogels
The co-assembly process and reversibility of chiral helical supramolecular hydrogels
The application of chiroptical supramolecular hydrogels

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Molecular structure of chiral hydrogelator (DG1 or LG1) and cartoon illustration of achiral carbon dots (Cdots and Pdots). b) Schematic illustration of chiral helical nanofibers self-assembled from DG1 without carbon dots(left), with Cdots (middle) and with Pdots (right), respectively. Only one type of chirality is described in panel b.

SEM images of M-helical nanofibers based on a) DG1 hydrogels, b) DG1-Cdots hydrogels, and c) DG1-Pdots hydrogels. SEM images of P-helical nanofibers based on d) LG1 hydrogels, e) LG1-Cdots hydrogels, and f) LG1-Pdots hydrogels. The insets show the magnified images of helical nanofibers and their helical pitch (P) and diameter (D). CLSM images of helical nanofibers based on g) DG1-Cdots and h) DG1-Pdots hydrogels. i-j) Statistical helical pitch and diameter of these helical nanofibers obtained without or with Cdots (or Pdots). k) Diagram of the relationship between helical pitch and diameter based on the chiral self-assembly of DG1 without or with Cdots (or Pdots), the insets show the corresponding photographs of hydrogels under natural light and UV light (365 nm), respectively. Only one type of chirality is described in diagram k.

CD and UV–vis spectra of a) DG1-Cdots and LG1-Cdots hydrogels, b) DG1-Pdots and LG1-Pdots hydrogels. Dissymmetry factor (g abs) for c) DG1-Cdots and LG1-Cdots hydrogels, d) DG1-Pdots and LG1-Pdots hydrogels. CPL spectra of e) DG1-Cdots and LG1-Cdots hydrogels, f) DG1-Pdots and LG1-Pdots hydrogels. Luminescence dissymmetry factor (g lum) for g) DG1-Cdots and LG1-Cdots hydrogels, h) DG1-Pdots and LG1-Pdots hydrogels (excited at 350 nm at ambient conditions).

a) Zeta potential of Cdots, Pdots, DG1 hydrogels, DG1-Cdots, and DG1-Pdots hydrogels. b) FTIR spectra of Cdots, PEI, Pdots, DG1-Cdots, and DG1-Pdots xerogels. c) XRD plots of Cdots and Pdots. d) XRD plots of DG1-Cdots and DG1-Pdots xerogels. e) XPS full survey of Cdots, Pdots, DG1-Cdots and DG1-Pdots xerogels. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectra of f) Cdots and Pdots, g) DG1-Cdots and DG1-Pdots xerogels. The high-resolution N1s XPS spectra of h) Cdots and Pdots and i) DG1-Cdots and DG1-Pdots xerogels.

a) SEM images of various nanostructures formed by DG1 and Pdots in aqueous solution at different time periods. The starting time point is set when the mixture is completely dissolved and the heat gun is removed. b) The corresponding time-dependent CD and UV–vis spectra of DG1-Pdots hydrogels. c) Schematic illustration of the gelation process of DG1 and Pdots. d) SEM image of helical nanofibers of DG1-Pdots after re-assembly. e) CD and UV–vis spectra of DG1-Pdots under cyclic disassembly and re-assembly treatment. f) Temperature-dependent CD and UV–vis spectra of DG1-Pdots hydrogels.

a) Cartoon of preparation of clear film. b-c) Clear film under incident light and UV light (365 nm), respectively. d) CPL spectra of clear films based on DG1-Pdots and LG1-Pdots hydrogels. e) Cartoon diagram of DG1-Pdots film upon irradiation with right- and left-handed circularly polarized light (350 nm), respectively. f) Photoemission spectra of DG1-Pdots film probed with left- and right-handed circularly polarized light (LCP and RCP, 350 nm), respectively. g) Photoemission spectra of LG1-Pdots film probed with LCP and RCP light, respectively.

head.ce:title: Chiral helical supramolecular hydrogels with adjustable pitch and diameter towards high-performance chiroptical detecting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Lab of Metal Matrix Composites, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai JiaoTong University, 800 Dongchuan Road, Shanghai 200240, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Lab of Metal Matrix Composites
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Lab of Metal Matrix Composites, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai JiaoTong University, 800 Dongchuan Road, Shanghai 200240, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Helical architectures with adjustable helical parameters and chiroptical functions are expected to play a fundamental role in biomedicine and nanoscience fields but is still in its early stage of study. Herein, chiral phenylalanine-derived hydrogelators (DG1 and LG1) can self-assemble into left-handed or right-handed helical nanofibrous hydrogels. Achiral carbon dots with different charges are co-assembled with DG1 or LG1 through electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds, helical supramolecular hydrogels with controllable helical pitch and diameter are readily obtained. Typically, the co-assembled hydrogels show both circular dichroism (CD) and circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) signals in the absorption regions. The nanofibers with the largest helical pitch and smallest diameter show the highest luminescence dissymmetry g-factors (g lum) up to -3.80 × 10−3, which are able to efficiently differentiate between left and right circularly polarized light. This work not only paves a universal method to obtain chiral helical nanostructures with adjustable pitch and diameter, but also shines light on these advanced helical nanostructures for potential applications in chiroptical detectors.

Negatively charged chiral phenylalanine-derived hydrogelators and positively charged carbon dots can co-assemble into nanofibrous supramolecular hydrogels. The degree of helical pitch and the diameter of these co-assembled nanofibers can be tailored by achiral carbon dots, thus further to affect their chiroptical performance.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Helical architectures with chiroptical properties [1-5] have drawn great attentions due to their wide potential applications in biosensors [6,7], chiral recognition [8-10], 3D displays [5,11], photovoltaic devices [12-15], and asymmetry catalysis [16-18]. Control of the helical parameters including dimension, size, shape, and handedness is crucial for the full exploitation of their potential applications, which has been extensively investigated by applying an external stimulus such as pH [19,20], light [21,22], temperature [23,24], chemical additives [25-28], solvent [29,30], and metal coordination [31-33]. However, precisely controlling helical pitch and diameter in helical supramolecular systems still is a significant challenge since these helices or twists are often obtained by serendipity [33]. Besides, there are much unknown about helical pitch and diameter how to regulate supramolecular chirality and circularly polarized luminescence (CPL). CPL reflects the chiroptical properties of chiral emitters at the excited state. CPL materials have recently received considerable attentions because of their fascinating potentials in 3D displays, information storage, photoelectric devices and photochemical reactions [5,34].

Chiral supramolecular systems co-assembled with different fluorescent inorganic nanomaterials provide a convenient platform to construct chiroptical helical architectures [35-40]. Representative works include supramolecular nanohelix of lanthanide-doped upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) [39] and perovskite nanocrystals embedded within a predefined handedness cholesteric superstructure stack [40]. However, controlling of the helical pitch and diameter of these hybrid helical nanostructures remains a challenge because it is difficult to regulate spatial arrangement of programmable building blocks in assembly process [5,34]. Besides, most of these hybrid helical architectures are obtained in organic solvents with undesirable biocompatibility, which limit their applications in biology [35]. These may be overcome by introducing carbon dots into supramolecular assembly systems since they have abundant hydrophilic groups (e.g. positively charged amino groups), which can provide modifiable active sites to participate in assembly process [41-43]. Moreover, carbon dots have the merits of easily functionalized, good biocompatibility, and good electronic and optical properties, which enable them to construct chiroptical helical architectures and can be used in the fields of biomedicine and nanoscience [44-49].

Herein, N-enriched carbon dots (Cdots) and branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) modified N-enriched carbon dots (Pdots) containing different positive charges are used to co-assemble with negatively charged chiral phenylalanine-derived hydrogelators (DG1 and LG1), respectively, and chiral helical nanofibrous hydrogels can be obtained. The helical pitch and diameter of these nanofibers can be well-regulated by the electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds between DG1 (or LG1) and Cdots or Pdots. The helical pitch of the nanofibers obtained in DG1 self-assembled hydrogels, DG1-Cdots, and DG1-Pdots co-assembled hydrogels are from around 180 nm, 130 nm, to 95 nm, respectively. The diameter of the nanofibers obtained in DG1 self-assembled hydrogels, DG1-Cdots, and DG1-Pdots co-assembled hydrogels are from around 55 nm, 75 nm, to 92 nm, respectively. Similar phenomena were observed in LG1-based hydrogels. DG1 and LG1 self-assembled hydrogels only show circular dichroism (CD) signals while co-assembled hydrogels show both CD and CPLsignals in the visible regions. Typically, the chiral nanofibers with the smallest helical pitch and the largest diameter lead to exhibiting highest luminescence dissymmetry g-factors (g lum) up to −3.80 × 10−3, which can be further used as a chiral detector to efficiently distinguish left and right circularly polarized light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Controllable pitch and diameter of helical nanofibers in supramolecular hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Cdots and Pdots were prepared via a simple hydrothermal treatment according to reported procedures and used for investigation (Scheme S1, Supporting Information) [49]. The morphologies of Cdots and Pdots were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The average particle sizes are about 6.0 nm for Cdots and 12.5 nm for Pdots, respectively (Figure S1, Supporting Information). The dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement results suggested that Cdots and Pdots had a hydrodynamic diameter of 65 ± 0.5 nm and 12.0 ± 0.5 nm in aqueous solution, respectively (Figure S2, Supporting Information). DG1 and LG1 were synthesized according to our previous reports, which could self-assemble into transparent nanofibrous hydrogels in aqueous solution [26,50]. Supramolecular co-assembled hydrogels were readily obtained by heating mixture of carbon dots (Cdots or Pdots) and DG1 or LG1 in aqueous solution. The effects of various concentrations of Cdots or Pdots (0.2 to 1.2 mg mL−1) on the co-assembled with DG1 was firstly studied and 0.6 mg mL−1 was selected as an optimal concentration (Figure S3, Supporting Information). The nanostructures of hydrogels were systematically investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). For DG1-based hydrogels, DG1, DG1-Cdots, and DG1-Pdots hydrogels all reveal left-handed (M) helical nanofibers (Fig. 1a-c). LG1, LG1-Cdots, and LG1-Pdots hydrogels all represent right-handed (P) helical nanofibers (Fig. 1d-f). The supramolecular chirality of hydrogels was controlled by the hydrogelators’ chirality [25]. TEM image of DG1-Pdots hydrogels shows that Pdots are well dispersed along the co-assembled helical nanofibers (Figure S4, Supporting Information). In laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM) images of DG1-Cdots and DG1-Pdots hydrogels, uniform long nanofibers showing bright blue emission are observed (Fig. 2g, h). It suggest that the added Cdots or Pdots can participate in the formation of helixes and do not change the helical nature of DG1[39,41].

The helical pitch and diameter of these helical nanofibers were further investigated (Fig. 1i-k, Figure S5-S6, Supporting Information). For DG1 hydrogels, a helical pitch is about 180 nm. A helical pitch is around 130 nm for DG1-Cdots hydrogels and the helical pitch of DG1-Pdots hydrogels is around 95 nm. Moreover, the diameter of DG1 hydrogels is about 55 nm, while the diameter of DG1-Cdots and DG1-Pdots hydrogels are around 75 nm and 92 nm, respectively. Similar phenomena were observed in LG1-based hydrogels. A helical pitch is about 175 nm for LG1 hydrogels, while the helical pitch of LG1-Cdots and LG1-Pdots are around 135 nm and 90 nm, respectively. The diameter of 50 nm for LG1 hydrogels while the diameter of 68 nm and 90 nm for LG1-Cdots and LG1-Pdots hydrogels, respectively (Fig. 1i, j). The results imply that both the helical pitch and diameter of chiral nanofibers are closely related to the achiral Cdots and Pdots (Fig. 1k) [19, 25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chiroptical activity of helical supramolecular hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Because DG1 and LG1 have chiral centers, the ground-state chiral characteristics of helical supramolecular hydrogels were checked by circular dichroism (CD) spectra in detail. The CD spectra of DG1 and LG1 hydrogels display mirror images peaks in the absorption region of 220–320 nm (Figure S7, Supporting Information). The UV–vis absorption spectra of Cdots and Pdots show analogous absorption peaks in the visible region of 200–250 nm (Figure S8, Supporting Information). Cdots and Pdots solutions are CD-silent due to its achiral nature (Figure S9, Supporting Information). The CD spectrum of DG1-Cdots hydrogels displays two positive peaks at around 265 and 310 nm, which ascribe to the induced supramolecular chirality of Cdots in M-helical aggregates after being co-assembled with DG1. Besides, the positive CD signal around 205–220 nm is attributed to the chiral stacking of D-type phenylalanine and Cdots chromophore. The CD spectrum of LG1-Cdots hydrogels displays opposite CD signals compared to DG1-Cdots hydrogels (Fig. 2a). The CD spectrum of DG1-Pdots hydrogels exhibits peaks in the 260–320 nm range, which ascribe to the induced supramolecular chirality of Pdots co-assembled with DG1 (Fig. 2b). The dissymmetry g-factor (g abs) was calculated by g abs = CD / (Abs × 32980) [5]. The g abs values at the maximum of CD spectra of DG1/LG1-Pdots hydrogels are stronger than that of DG1/LG1-Cdots hydrogels (Fig. 2c, d), which suggest that the chiral transfer between DG1 and Pdots is better than that DG1 and Cdots during co-assembly [25-28].

The fluorescence (FL) spectra of Cdots and Pdots both exhibit single emission peak around 445 nm under 365 nm excitation (Figure S8, Supporting Information). FL spectra of the co-assembled hydrogels have the same shapes as those recorded for aqueous solutions of Cdots and Pdots (Figure S10, Supporting Information), which indicate that the fundamental emission properties of Cdots and Pdots are well preserved in the co-hydrogels [39,41]. Cdots and Pdots solutions are CPL-silent due to its achiral nature (Figure S11, Supporting Information). The excited-state chirality of co-assembled hydrogels was measured by CPL spectra in detail. A negative CPL signal around 445 nm is observed for DG1-Cdots hydrogels whereas a positive CPL signal is recorded for LG1-Cdots hydrogels. Similar, a negative CPL signal around 445 nm is observed for DG1-Pdots hydrogels, whereas a positive CPL signal is detected for LG1-Pdots hydrogels (Fig. 2e, f). The handedness of CPL of the co-assembled hydrogels is dependent on the chirality of hydrogels templates [41]. To quantitatively discuss the degree of CPL, the luminescence dissymmetry factor (g lum) was calculated by the equation of g lum = 2 × [ellipticity/ (32980/ln10)]/total fluorescence intensity at the CPL extremum peak [5]. The strongest CPL intensity is obtained in DG1-Pdots hydrogels with g lum of −3.80 × 10−3, which is stronger than that of DG1-Cdots hydrogels (Fig. 2g, h). These g lum values are considerably high as compared with other pure organic supramolecular systems [5]. All the above results indicate that the chirality of DG1 or LG1 can be transferred to the co-assembled hydrogels and the intensity of CPL can be regulated by Cdots and Pdots [39,41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The driving forces for the construction of chiral helical supramolecular hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The driving forces for the construction of chiroptical supramolecular hydrogels were investigated in details. Pdots have positive charges (zeta potentials: +42.2 mV) from zeta potential analysis, which is higher than that of Cdots (zeta potentials: +17.6 mV) due to their surface modification of cationic branched PEI. DG1 hydrogels have negative charges (zeta potential: −38.9 mV). DG1-Cdots hydrogels have less negative charges compared to DG1 hydrogels due to the electrostatic interactions between DG1 and Cdots. DG1-Pdots hydrogels have almost no charges compared to DG1-Cdots hydrogels due to the existence of stronger electrostatic interactions between DG1 and Pdots (Fig. 3 a) [19]. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) was employed to obtain insight about the interactions of these samples [25]. FTIR spectrum of Cdots shows a broad band centered around 3436 cm−1 assign to OH and NH groups. The strong peaks of 1660 cm−1, 1560 cm−1, 1617 cm−1, and 1309 cm−1 are attributed to amide I (C = O), amide II (NH), C = C, and CN, respectively (Fig. 3b and Figure S12, Supporting Information). The FTIR spectrum of Pdots exhibits similar peaks compared to the spectrum of Cdots, however, the absorption of amide I (C = O) at 1660 cm−1 become stronger and the amide II (NH) peak at 1560 cm−1 shows a declining trend for the spectrum of Pdots, suggesting more amide groups are formed since PEI molecules are chemically grafted onto the surface of Cdots [49]. The carboxylic (COOH) band of DG1-Cdots xerogels is around 1730 cm−1 with an obvious decrease compared with that of DG1 xerogels, suggesting hydrogen bonds form between carboxylic acid of DG1 and amino groups of Cdots. Moreover, well-defined amide I (C = O) and amide II (NH) bands centered at 1638 cm−1 and 1540 cm−1 are observed, respectively, indicating that amide groups participate in the formation of hydrogen bonds. FTIR spectrum of DG1-Pdots xerogels show well-defined amide I (C = O) and amide II (NH) bands center around 1638 cm−1 and 1540 cm−1, respectively, and the carboxylic groups (C = O) at 1730 cm−1 almost disappears, showing stronger hydrogen bonds form between DG1 and Pdots compared to DG1-Cdots xerogels. The results suggest that electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds between DG1 and Pdots are stronger than those between DG1 and Cdots. We propose that stronger electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds between DG1 and Pdots leading to shorter inter-molecular distances, resulting in the co-assemblies remaining closer, thus resulting in shorter pitch and larger diameter of helices [25,26].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed to unveil the molecular arrangement of samples. XRD patterns of Cdots and Pdots both show a broad diffraction peak around 24°, suggesting an amorphous nature of Cdots and Pdots, which corresponds to the graphitic structures (Fig. 3c) [42-44]. The XRD pattern of DG1-Cdots xerogels do not significantly change compared with the XRD pattern ofDG1-Pdots xerogels, suggesting they have the same molecular stacking patterns in the presence of Cdots or Pdots (Fig. 3d). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used to further investigate the surface group of Cdots, Pdots, DG1-Cdots, and DG1-Pdots xerogels. The full survey of XPS of all samples show three typical peaks: C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, indicating that they are mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements [42-45]. In the high-resolution C1s XPS spectra of Cdots and Pdots, the C1s band can be de-convoluted into four peaks, corresponding to sp2 carbons (C = C/CC, 284.6 eV), sp3 carbons (CN, 285.1 eV), sp3 carbons (CO, 286.9 eV), and carbonyl carbons (C = O, 288.5 eV). It shows the content of CN and CO significantly increase from Cdots to Pdots. Similar, in the high-resolution C1s XPS spectra of DG1-Cdots and DG1-Pdots xerogels, the C1s band can be de-convoluted into four peaks too, matching to sp2 carbons (C = C/CC, 284.7 eV), sp3 carbons (CN, 285.6 eV), sp3 carbons (CO, 286.2 eV), and carbonyl carbons (C = O, 288.5 eV). In the high-resolution N1s XPS spectra of Cdots and Pdots, the N1s band can be converted into pyridone N (400.1 eV) and C N + (401.4 eV), respectively. In the high-resolution N1s XPS spectra of DG1-Cdots and DG1-Pdots xerogels, the N1s band can be converted into N (-NH-, 399.8 eV) and N (C N +, 401.5 eV), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The co-assembly process and reversibility of chiral helical supramolecular hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: To track the evolution process of the co-assembled hydrogels morphologies, the nanostructures of DG1-Pdots formed at various time points were studied by SEM (Fig. 4 a) [50]. Initially, few nanoribbons without obvious chirality are obtained within 5 min. After 30 min, with increasing the co-assembly time, the nanoribbons grow into longer right-handed helical nanofibers. By 60 min, many mature right-handed helical nanofibers emerge. Moreover, the morphology of the helical nanofibers remains unchanged after one week, suggesting that the supramolecular chirality of DG1-Pdots hydrogels can be kept without destruction (Fig. 4a) [35]. Meanwhile, the formation of DG1-Pdots hydrogels was further monitored by time-dependent CD and UV–vis spectroscopy (Fig. 4b). The CD signal of DG1-Pdots hydrogels is almost indiscernible in the initial stage (< 5 min). The situation dramatically changed between 10 to 60 min when a series of sharp positive Cotton effects emerge. After 60 min, the increase in the CD intensity virtually cease. Moreover, the CD intensity of the final DG1-Pdots hydrogels remains unchanged after one week, which are consistent with the results of SEM. The helical conformation of co-assembled hydrogels is sensitive to external stimuli due to the existence of electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds [51-53]. The reversibility of the DG1-Pdots was investigated after multiple cycles of assembly and disassembly (Fig. 4c-e). As a result of thermal-controlled assembly and dissociation of the DG1-Pdots nanoassemblies, cyclical changes in the CD and SEM were observed. The CD signals remain constant approximately 25 °C and continuously decrease at higher temperatures. The optical activity eventually vanished around 95 °C (Fig. 4f), and the nanofibers dissociate into relatively small irregular nanoparticles (Figure S13, Supporting Information). The CPL spectra of the disassembled state of the DG1-Pdots mixtures were also tested. Although the mixture exhibited strong blue emission under UV excitation, no CPL signs are observed (Figure S14, Supporting Information). It appears that the integrity of the co-assembled nanostructure is essential for expressing the chiroptical properties of supramolecular hydrogels [50,51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of chiroptical supramolecular hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the potentials of the prepared chiral helical supramolecular hydrogels in chiroptical device [54-60], we further used the above chiral helical supramolecular hydrogels (taking DG1-Pdots and LG1-Pdots hydrogels as examples) to prepare composite films taking polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as supporting material (Fig. 5 a). After being dried under ambient conditions, a free-standing and transparent film (DG1-Pdots or LG1-Pdots film) with a uniform thickness was obtained (Fig. 5b). The film exhibits strong blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm, Fig. 5c). In CPL spectra, DG1-Pdots film shows a negative sign while LG1-Pdots film shows a positive signal (Fig. 5d), which are consistent with the handedness of the results in corresponding supramolecular hydrogels. The g lum values in the composite films are about +3.60 × 10−3 and −3.70 × 10−3, respectively.These results suggest that high chiroptical performances are kept in the composite films. Photoemission spectra of the composite films probed with left- and right-handed circularly polarized (LCP and RCP, 350 nm) light were studied to explore the composite films of responsiveness to LCP and RCP light (Fig. 5f, g). For DG1-Pdots film, the photoemission intensity detected by LCP light is higher than that by RCP light, which means DG1-Pdots film selective transmission of LCP light without much attenuation. In contrast, for LG1-Pdots film, the photoemission intensity detected by RCP light is higher than that by LCP light, which means LG1-Pdots film selective transmission of RCP light without much attenuation. DG1-Pdots film become clear upon illumination with LCP light, while, it appears dark upon illumination with RCP light (Fig. 5e). The results suggest that differentiating between LCP and RCP light can be achieved by DG1-Pdots or LG1-Pdots films due to its predominant left-handed or right-handed helical organizations [54-55].

In summary, chiral helical supramolecular hydrogels with adjustable pitch and diameter had been successfully fabricated by the co-assembly of negatively charged chiral hydrogelators and positively charged achiral carbon dots. The co-assembled hydrogels showed both CD and CPL signals in the absorption regions. Typically, the nanofibers with the smallest helical pitch and the largest diameter had the highest g lum values, which could be used as a chiral detector to efficiently distinguish between left and right circularly polarized light. This work provides an efficient and generic method for the fabrication of helical supramolecular polymers with adjustable helical pitch and diameter and hence may open an innovative route to develop high-performance chiroptical materials.

The powder of DG1(3.0 mg), Cdots (0.6 mg mL−1, 1.0 mL), and deionized water (0.2 mL) were mixed in a 5 mL seal-capped glass vial. The aqueous suspension was heated (≈100 °C) by a heat gun until the solid was completely dissolved to give a transparent solution. When the mixture was cooled to room temperature, a self-supporting hydrogel formed within a few minutes. The formation of DG1-Cdots hydrogel was confirmed with the vial inversion method. For DG1-Pdots hydrogels, the process was the same as above.

Typically, 1.0 mL of Pdots (0.6 mg mL−1), 3.0 mg of DG1, 0.04 mg of PVA, and deionized water (0.2 mL) were added into a 5 mL seal-capped glass vial to form the mixture solutions. Then, the mixture solutions were added into a plastic Petri dish (60 × 15 mm) to allow solvent evaporation-induced self-assembly in an ambient environment. After being dried, a free-standing chiral film can be obtained by peeling off.
Supplementary materials associated with this article can be found, in the online version.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (51833006, 52003154), the Innovation Program of Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (201701070002E00061), the Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (20S31904600, 19ZR1425400), Shanghai Pujiang Program (20PJ1407400) and Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (20ZR1425500).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.giant.2021.100077.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Snir, R.D. Kamien, Entropically driven helix Formation, Science307 (2005) 1067.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Formation of functional super-helical assemblies by constrained single heptad repeat
